channel.title: NACLA – News & Analysis + Reviews (English)
channel.description: English-language articles and reviews from NACLA

item.title: Desiring Temporalities in the Face of Permanent War
item.description: The Summer 2026 issue of the NACLA Report explores artistic and cultural practices that activate memory, engage the archive, and expand the possibilities of collective life.

item.title: The Violent Legacies Haunting Contemporary Guatemala
item.description: The unchecked power of multinational corporations, state-criminal networks, and legacies of genocide against Indigenous communities continue to fuel violence against public figures in the country.

item.title: Colombia Joins the Far-Right Wave
item.description: Abelardo de la Espriella, a far-right lawyer with ties to paramilitary groups, is set to win a historically close election, a result that threatens to repeat the mistakes of Colombia’s past.

item.title: I’ll Samba Someplace Else: A Spatial History of Race, Ethnicity and Displacement in São Paulo (Review)
item.description: In his book, Andrew G. Britt explores the ways in which urban planning was used as a tool to erase São Paulo’s once vibrant black neighborhoods.

item.title: Colombia’s Land Reform is At Stake
item.description: A far-right victory in upcoming elections could reverse hard-fought gains for victims of the armed conflict, including survivors of one of Colombia’s most notorious paramilitary massacres.

item.title: States of Defeat: U.S. Imaginaries of Revolutionary Central America (Review)
item.description: In his new book, Eric A. Vázquez looks at how solidarity in Central America has evolved through time.

item.title: Miguel Krassnoff and the Memory Question in Chile
item.description: New charges against one of the Pinochet dictatorship’s most prominent human rights abusers come as the country shifts markedly to the right, casting historical memory in doubt.

item.title: Getting Sanctions Wrong: Why Recent Claims About Venezuela Just Don’t Add Up
item.description: A recent book treats economic sanctions as a tool of benevolent intervention, an argument justified by a series of egregious analytical errors.

item.title: Colombia Debates “Total Peace” and “Total War”
item.description: The June 21 presidential runoff will feature starkly different approaches to the growing power of armed and criminal groups.

item.title: A Far-Right Surge and a Historic Runoff in Colombia
item.description: Abelardo de la Espriella’s first-round victory demonstrated the strength of the country’s surging populist far-right, putting the left in a bind.

item.title: Impunity and the Permanent Coup in Peru
item.description: A massacre in a peripheral area of Peru highlights how the state has built a system that perpetuates impunity.

item.title: A Flotilla Exposes the Dutch Caribbean’s Struggle for Climate Justice
item.description: Despite co-hosting a milestone climate conference, the Netherlands has left the Dutch Caribbean islands alone in their fight against climate change.

item.title: HondurasGate | Under the Shadow, Season 2, Episode 11
item.description: Michael Fox walks you through the HondurasGate leaks. What they are, what they mean, and the repercussions for the region.

item.title: Leonardo Padura’s Like Dust in the Wind and the Afterlife of a Generation
item.description: Padura’s novel traces the dreams, disappointments, and dispersal of a generation shaped by the Cuban Revolution.

item.title: Could Anti-Fujimorismo Prevail Again?
item.description: As Peru heads toward another polarized runoff, the country remains caught between two competing visions of the national future.

item.title: Bolivia into the Abyss
item.description: Fuel shortages, inflation, and political exclusion have triggered a wave of fragmented unrest with no clear movement capable of channeling popular anger.

item.title: Learning Mandarin, Riding the Metro: China’s Street-Level Presence in Bogotá
item.description: China’s presence in Colombia is no longer an abstract geopolitical discussion but rather a reality negotiated day by day through construction work, commercial exchange, language learning, and personal relationships.

item.title: Music and Political Imprisonment in Pinochet’s Chile (Review)
item.description: Katia Chornik’s book details how music was used as a form of torture and resistance during the Pinochet dictatorship.

item.title: The Essequibo Illusion
item.description: A 185-year-old territorial dispute between Venezuela and Guyana reveals the enduring contradictions of U.S. foreign policy in the Caribbean, with the “Donroe Doctrine” introducing fresh diplomatic risks.

item.title: Web Series: Haiti’s Path to Liberation
item.description: These articles offer new ways of thinking about Haiti and a powerful vision and approach rooted in the country’s culture and history that can build a different future.

item.title: Haiti and the Black Radical Tradition
item.description: Haiti’s revolutionary legacy offers a blueprint for coalition-building, political struggle, and mutual investment in the age of Trump.

item.title: Re-Haitianizing Haitian Freedom: What Will It Take?
item.description: A framework for confronting imperial narratives and rebuilding Haiti through local knowledge and collective power.

item.title: Parables of Empire & Unfreedom in Haiti Today
item.description: Forty years after the fall of Jean-Claude Duvalier, Haiti’s ongoing crisis exposes the devastating legacy of permanent foreign intervention.

item.title: La motosierra contra el pensamiento: ciencia pública, autoritarismo y erosión democrática en Argentina
item.description: Los ataques del gobierno de extrema derecha contra las instituciones públicas y el conocimiento que producen han debilitado a los trabajadores, la economía, y la democracia de Argentina.

item.title: A Chainsaw Against Thought: Public Science, Authoritarianism, and the Erosion of Democracy in Argentina
item.description: The far-right government’s attacks on public institutions and the knowledge they produce have weakened Argentina’s workers, economy, and democracy.

item.title: Call for Pitches | Narcoterrorism, State Power, and Transnational Criminal Organizations Reconsidered
item.description: [CLOSED] NACLA is currently accepting proposals for our Winter 2026 issue on state violence, insecurity, and the political production of “criminality.”

item.title: Lucha, Play, and Possibility: Reimagining Mexican American Stories Through Comics
item.description: John Avina’s comics, set in Chicago’s Pilsen neighborhood, re-imagine Mexican American life beyond conquest narratives, deportation regimes, and generational loss.

item.title: Building the “Economía para la vida”
item.description: At a historic conference in Bogotá, heterodox thinkers and politicians gathered to debate the construction of a new international economic order oriented around human life and dignity.

item.title: The Letters of Minerva Mirabal and Manolo Tavárez: Love and Resistance in the Time of Trujillo (Review)
item.description: Minou Tavárez Mirabal’s 2022 book sheds light on the complex lives of her parents, two Dominican revolutionaries who were assassinated for their political activism.

item.title: The Amazon’s Invisible Crisis: How Environmental Crime Is Reshaping Humanitarian Emergencies
item.description: As environmental crimes threaten Indigenous communities in the Amazon, their struggles are ignored by a humanitarian system that has rendered such emergencies invisible.

item.title: The New Litmus Test for Sovereign Governance: ISDS Arbitration
item.description: The “Donroe Doctrine” is compelling Latin American governments into arbitration courts that benefit large multinational corporations, with limited success.

item.title: At a Historic Conference, Frontline Groups Push for a Just Transition
item.description: Though the First Conference on Transitioning away from Fossil Fuels is a historic achievement, movement organizers question if it will create needed pathways toward a just transition.

item.title: A Political Turn in Honduras Endangers Maya Chorti Culture
item.description: Recent elections in Honduras have returned right-wing leadership to power, with immediate implications for the autonomy of Indigenous communities.

item.title: Militarizing Ecuador’s Prisons has Failed
item.description: Two years on, far-right President Daniel Noboa’s prison crackdown—characterized by militarization, corruption, and deadly violence—has yielded no results.

item.title: Bukele, El rey desnudo (Review)
item.description: Óscar Martínez’s profile of Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele unmasks an authoritarian leader who hangs on to power through propaganda, gang pacts, and repression.

item.title: The 2026 World Cup, ICE, and the Long Shadow of USA ‘94
item.description: The 2026 “Save the World Cup Act” Stirs Up Hidden Histories of Latino Immigration Activism during USA ‘94.

item.title: Review of Migrant Struggles in Times of Pandemic and Crisis: A View from the Americas
item.description: In a continent marked by multiple overlapping crises, Migrant Struggles in Times of Pandemic and Crisis: A View from the Americas emerges as an urgent and deeply necessary work.

item.title: Lessons from the Zapatistas: From Armed Insurgency to People’s Autonomy (Review)
item.description: A new book summarizes nearly 30 years of Zapatista political activity, tracing the construction of Mayan self-governance, unpacking the insurgency’s “critical theory,” and drawing lessons for the present.

item.title: Conservation in Colombia Leaves Locals Behind
item.description: As the government moves to establish a coast guard station in Gorgona Island, local communities claim they have been left out of the process.

item.title: How U.S. Policy Fueled Mexico’s Cartel Crisis | Under the Shadow Season 2, Episode 10
item.description: Drugs. They have been the boogeyman across Latin America for decades. An excuse that has led to so much violence. And Trump is turning up the temperature.

item.title: Mexico and the Hegemon’s Handcuffs
item.description: From the Bracero crisis of 1954 to today’s cartel wars, Mexico has framed subordination as partnership, though history suggests that room to maneuver exists—albeit within limits set by Washington.

item.title: Argentina’s Glaciers are Under Threat
item.description: As the country’s far-right government attempts to undo groundbreaking legislation that has protected glaciers for 15 years, activists are fighting back.

item.title: From Hope to Uncertainty: Venezuelans in Far-Right Chile
item.description: In January, Venezuelans in Chile briefly celebrated the fall of Nicolás Maduro, but many now fear a harsher climate for migrants as the country shifts to the right.

item.title: In His Father’s Name, an Indigenous Man Takes on the Government for Abuses at the Krenak Reformatory
item.description: An Indigenous man in Brazil endured torture and forced labor for 19 months in a dictatorship “concentration camp”. Fifty-five years later, his son seeks justice.

item.title: Counter-Archives to Border Fascism
item.description: Even as borders harden, life continues to exceed them. The Spring 2026 issue of the NACLA Report traces how border violence has moved from the margins to the center of political life across the Americas.

item.title: The FBI’s Return to Ecuador Is Nothing New
item.description: The announcement of a permanent FBI office in Quito is the latest iteration of the U.S. using security cooperation to intervene in the country’s domestic politics.

item.title: Milei’s War against the Working Class
item.description: Argentina’s sweeping labor reform, heavily resisted in the streets, is set to roll back nearly a century of hard-fought rights.

item.title: Feminist Fútbol and Historical Memory in Argentina
item.description: As Argentina marks 50 years since the beginning of its last bloody dictatorship, a feminist fútbol club in Buenos Aires keeps the memory of the disappeared alive as the government fosters negationism.

item.title: Cuba’s Cosmopolitan Enclaves: Imperialism and Internationalism in Eastern Sugar Towns (Review)
item.description: A new book places multiracial sugar workers in Cuba’s northeast at the vanguard of inter-war internationalism, solidarity, and anti-imperialism.

item.title: Will the Coca Leaf Ever Lose its Stigma?
item.description: The World Health Organization’s recent refusal to reclassify coca leaf is yet another failure to understand the plant’s cultural significance.
